meta.question: Could the LHC create a black hole that destroys Earth?

positions.label: No risk — LHC collisions are safe
Residual concern — the safety argument has weak or speculative links

datasets.label: Cosmic-ray empirical bound (white-dwarf & neutron-star survival)

confirmation.note: Audit metadata backfill: preserves the case's prior curator-confirmed decision; the source is a direct supporting source, not a verified dependency quote.

datasets.proposition: Cosmic rays above LHC energies have struck the Earth, Sun, white dwarfs and neutron stars for billions of years without destroying them; if LHC collisions could make a dangerous stable object, these dense stars would already be gone — an empirical upper bound on the risk.

datasets.label: Hawking radiation (black-hole evaporation)

confirmation.note: Audit metadata backfill: preserves the case's prior curator-confirmed decision; the source is a direct supporting source, not a verified dependency quote.

datasets.proposition: Any microscopic black hole produced would radiate away and evaporate essentially instantly via Hawking radiation, so it could never accrete.

confirmation.note: Audit metadata backfill: preserves the case's prior curator-confirmed decision; the source is a direct supporting source, not a verified dependency quote.

datasets.label: Argument-reliability analysis (probability the safety argument itself is flawed)

confirmation.note: Audit metadata backfill: preserves the case's prior curator-confirmed decision; the source is a direct supporting source, not a verified dependency quote.

datasets.proposition: The safety case is a chain of theoretical assumptions; the probability the argument as a whole is flawed is not negligible and is itself a residual risk.

datasets.label: Metastable-black-hole accretion scenario

confirmation.note: Audit metadata backfill: preserves the case's prior curator-confirmed decision; the source is a direct supporting source, not a verified dependency quote.

datasets.proposition: In some beyond-standard-model scenarios a metastable micro black hole might accrete rather than evaporate, so the no-evaporation branch is not fully excluded.

datasets.label: Production impossibility under standard gravity
datasets.proposition: LHC collision energies (~10 TeV) are ~15 orders of magnitude below the Planck scale, so black-hole production is impossible in standard 4D gravity — it requires speculative large extra dimensions.

confirmation.note: Audit metadata backfill: preserves the case's prior curator-confirmed decision; the source is a direct supporting source, not a verified dependency quote.

datasets.label: Accretion-timescale safety argument
datasets.proposition: Even a stable, neutral micro black hole would accrete matter far too slowly to grow dangerous within the lifetime of the Earth or the Sun.

confirmation.note: Audit metadata backfill: preserves the case's prior curator-confirmed decision; the source is a direct supporting source, not a verified dependency quote.

factors.label: Reliability of the cosmic-ray safety analogy
factors.rationale: The entire empirical safety case rests on cosmic-ray-produced black holes not having already destroyed dense stars.

provenance.quote: eight observed white dwarf stars... present a form of living evidence that the LHC must be safe
the cosmic-ray argument relies on assumptions that could themselves be mistaken
cosmic-ray collisions at energies far above those of any accelerator

factors.label: Whether Hawking radiation is guaranteed (black-hole evaporation)
factors.rationale: Deliberately builds a safety argument that does not depend on Hawking evaporation.

provenance.quote: even if Hawking radiation does not exist
microscopic black holes would decay almost instantly by Hawking radiation
a metastable black hole could grow rather than evaporate

factors.label: Probability the safety argument itself is flawed
factors.rationale: The core residual-concern move: the reported ~0 risk ignores the error rate of the safety argument.

provenance.quote: their argument may fail for reasons such as a flaw in the underlying theory, a mistake in modelling, or a calculation error
reassurances may be motivated more by the wish to proceed
do not adequately account for the probability that the underlying arguments are themselves mistaken

factors.label: Independence of the safety review from the institution running the experiment

provenance.quote: safety reviews were conducted by the same institution seeking to run the experiment

sources.title: Giddings & Mangano — Astrophysical implications of hypothetical stable TeV-scale black holes

position.quote: In short, this study finds no basis for concerns that TeV-scale black holes from the LHC could pose a risk to Earth on time scales shorter than the Earth's natural lifetime.

sources.title: LHC Safety Assessment Group (LSAG) — Review of the Safety of LHC Collisions
sources.venue: Journal of Physics G: Nuclear and Particle Physics

position.quote: LHC collisions present no danger and there are no reasons for concern

sources.title: Ellis, Giudice, Mangano, Tkachev, Wiedemann — Review of the Safety of LHC Collisions (J. Phys. G)

position.quote: The stability of astronomical bodies constrains strongly the possible rate of accretion by any such microscopic black holes, so that they present no conceivable danger.

sources.title: Hawking — Particle creation by black holes

position.quote: This thermal emission leads to a slow decrease in the mass of the black hole and to its eventual disappearance: any primordial black hole of mass less than about 10 15 g would have evaporated by now.

sources.title: Koch, Bleicher, Stöcker — Exclusion of black hole disaster scenarios at the LHC

position.quote: In this paper we summarize the most straight forward arguments that are necessary to rule out such doomsday scenarios.

sources.title: Ord, Hillerbrand & Sandberg — Probing the Improbable: Methodological Challenges for Risks with Low Probabilities and High Stakes

position.quote: Using the risk estimates from the Large Hadron Collider as a test case, we show how serious the problem can be when it comes to catastrophic risks and how best to address it.

sources.title: Calogero — Might a laboratory experiment destroy planet Earth?

position.quote: there is a tendency to underplay such risks... reassurances may be motivated more by the wish to proceed than by the strength of the case

sources.title: Johnson — The Black Hole Case: The Injunction Against the End of the World (Wagner & Sancho lawsuit)

position.quote: Next, the article explores the daunting challenges the case presents to equity, evidence, and law-and-economics analysis.

sources.title: CERN — The Safety of the LHC (public statement)

position.quote: A report published today in the peer reviewed Journal of Physics G: Nuclear and Particle Physics provides comprehensive evidence that safety fears about the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) are unfounded.

sources.title: Hut & Rees — How stable is our vacuum? (cosmic rays as a natural experiment)

position.quote: We show here that this chance, fortunately, is completely negligible since the region inside our past light cone has already survived some 10 5 cosmic ray collisions at centre of mass energies of 10 11 GeV and higher.

sources.title: Tegmark & Bostrom — Is a doomsday catastrophe likely?

position.quote: Here we derive a new upper bound of one per billion years (99.9% confidence level) for the exogenous terminal-catastrophe rate that is free of such selection bias, using calculations based on the relatively late formation time of Earth.

sources.title: Blaizot et al. — Study of potentially dangerous events during heavy-ion collisions at the LHC

position.quote: we find no basis for any conceivable threat from heavy-ion collisions at the LHC

sources.title: Casadio, Fabi & Harms — On the catastrophic black hole growth scenario at the LHC

position.quote: Based on this analysis, we argue against the possibility of catastrophic black hole growth at the LHC.

sources.title: Cavaglià — Particle accelerators as black hole factories?

position.quote: any microscopic black hole would evaporate almost instantaneously via Hawking radiation

sources.title: Plaga — On the potential catastrophic risk from metastable quantum black holes produced at colliders

position.quote: A plausible scenario in which these black holes accrete ambient matter at the Eddington limit shortly after their production, thereby emitting Hawking radiation that would be harmful to Earth and/or CERN and its surroundings, is described.

sources.title: Giddings & Mangano — Comments on claimed risk from metastable black holes (response to Plaga)

position.quote: We comment on inconsistency of this proposed scenario.

sources.title: Kent — A critical look at risk assessments for global catastrophes

position.quote: Future policy on catastrophe risks would be more rational, and more deserving of public trust, if acceptable risk bounds were generally agreed upon ahead of time and if serious research on whether those bounds could indeed be guaranteed was carried out well in advance of any hypothetically risky experiment, with the relevant debates involving experts with no stake in the experiments under consideration.
